coredata.dc:title: Plastic antibodies integrated with graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of bisphenol-A in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Singhal, Ayushi
Khan, Raju
coredata.dc:description: Bisphenol-A (BPA) is a hazardous chemical used in plastic manufacturing units. BPA is recognized as an endocrine disruptor that can act as environmental estrogen and affect the activity of natural endogenous hormones. BPA is both genotoxic and cytotoxic; hence, developing a sensor for its recognition and quantification is essential. In this study, a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) based electrochemical sensor was developed for the analysis of Bisphenol-A (BPA). The fabrication of BPA/PEDOT/GQDs/AuNPs/GCE was carried out by electrodepositing the gold (Au) nanoparticles followed by electropolymerization employing 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) and in-situ graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The characterization was performed using numerous techniques such as FESEM, XRD, UV–vis, TEM, FTIR, and electrochemical techniques. The linear range for the detection of Bisphenol-A was 1 nM to 50 µM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.19 nM and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.64 nM. The proposed method delivered selective, sensitive, and reusable electrodes for the detection of BPA. The recovery was found to be 95–112% and the fabricated electrode was successfully utilized for the analysis of BPA in the real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenol-A
Electrochemical
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Graphene quantum dots
Biomimetic
Sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electroanalytical evaluation of the molecularly imprinted sensor
Determination of BPA in spiked tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the (A) hydrothermal synthesis of graphene quantum dots, (B) fabrication process involved in the development of MIP-based sensor for BPA.
(A) UV–vis (B) Raman spectra (C) FTIR (D) XRD (E) TEM and (F) HR-TEM images of GQDs respectively. FESEM images of electropolymerized PEDOT layer (G) before and (H) after the extraction of BPA.

(A) DPV signal responses of the fabricated electrode to various concentrations of BPA (B) calibration plot of the fabricated sensor. Specificity of (C) imprinted electrode and (D) non-imprinted electrode.
Identification of BPA in tap water.

head.ce:title: Plastic antibodies integrated with graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of bisphenol-A in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Industrial Waste Utilization, Nano and Biomaterials
CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphenol-A (BPA) is a hazardous chemical used in plastic manufacturing units. BPA is recognized as an endocrine disruptor that can act as environmental estrogen and affect the activity of natural endogenous hormones. BPA is both genotoxic and cytotoxic; hence, developing a sensor for its recognition and quantification is essential. In this study, a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) based electrochemical sensor was developed for the analysis of Bisphenol-A (BPA). The fabrication of BPA/PEDOT/GQDs/AuNPs/GCE was carried out by electrodepositing the gold (Au) nanoparticles followed by electropolymerization employing 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) and in-situ graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The characterization was performed using numerous techniques such as FESEM, XRD, UV–vis, TEM, FTIR, and electrochemical techniques. The linear range for the detection of Bisphenol-A was 1 nM to 50 µM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.19 nM and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.64 nM. The proposed method delivered selective, sensitive, and reusable electrodes for the detection of BPA. The recovery was found to be 95–112% and the fabricated electrode was successfully utilized for the analysis of BPA in the real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol-A (BPA) is recognized as an endocrine-disruptive substance found in the nearby environment. BPA can disturb the health of humans and other organisms by interfering with the functioning of the endocrine system [1–4].

There is a crucial need to develop simple, rapid, economical, and sensitive methods to detect and monitor BPA for the well-being of public health. Electrochemical techniques have shown tremendous potential in the biosensing field, as they are simple, cost-effective, quick, and sensitive. Electrochemical techniques utilize biorecognition elements such as antibodies, antigens, enzymes, aptamers, or molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [5,6]. MIPs are the artificial polymeric receptor also known as plastic antibodies. The MIP-based sensors have a low-cost synthesis process and are reliable, selective, sensitive, thermally, and physically stable for an extended period. Additionally, MIP-based materials can be created synthetically. Unlike the traditional biorecognition components, they can be produced in huge quantities and do not need to be stored at a specific temperature or under freezing. For qualitative and quantitative reasons, the MIP-based sensor can be utilized to find minute quantities of analyte in actual samples [7,8]. The polymerization of the monomers occurs in the existence of a template that entraps the template molecule in a polymetric network. The functional monomers maintain the complementary imprinted cavities in the desired position after extracting the template molecule from the polymer matrix [9,10]. MIP based nanosensor has been used by many researchers for the determination of various analytes such as l-phenylalanine [11], glucose [12], carbendazim [13], 5-hydroxymethyl furfural [14]. In the present study, a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor modified with PEDOT/GQDs/AuNPs composite was synthesized for the determination of BPA in water samples. AuNPs have been used due to their high conductivity, high surface area, and superior binding capacities with analytes [15]. Unique properties such as wide and easy functionalization and the possibility to use them as composite can enhance or control the heterogeneous electron transfer. The functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or epoxy groups, make GQDs dispersible in an aqueous solution and contribute to the electrochemical activity of graphene-based materials [16]. The proposed sensor showed good stability, and good selectivity with good recovery.

The GQDs were synthesized as per the method reported by Chen Weifeng, et al. with little modifications [17]. The synthesis steps for GQDs are shown in Fig. 1
(A). The process of the development of the MIP-based electrode occurred in three steps as shown in Fig. 1 (B). The graphs obtained at the different steps for the fabrication of the modified electrode are shown in Fig. S1 .

The optical properties of the synthesized GQDs were examined using UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 2
(A) shows the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs. The spectrum shows a definite peak at ∼ 283 nm which is assigned to the n-π* transition due to the presence of a polyaromatic structure [18]. On giving excitation to the GQDs sample inside the UV cabinet, the green luminescence of GQDs appeared. The inset of Fig. 2 (A) shows the image of GQDs irradiated under UV light and daylight. Fig. 2 (B) shows the Raman spectrum of the GQDs. The Raman spectra show two characteristic bands (D, G). The peak was assigned to the D band at 1393 cm−1 and the G band at 1582 cm−1. The intensity ratio 0.88 of the D to G band confirms GQDs have a similar structure to graphite and are highly crystalline in nature and are 1–3 layers of graphene [18]. To determine the functionality found in the GQDs, an FTIR study was conducted ( Fig. 2 (C)). The FTIR results display the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups which show the occurrence of oxygen groups on the surface of materials at 1636 cm−1 (C = O), 2921 cm−1 (C-H), 1402 cm−1 (C = C), 1353 cm−1 (C-H), 1014 cm−1 (C-O) and the band near 3258 cm−1 (OH) rising due to water molecular. The oxygen-containing groups indicate the high solubility of GQDs in water [19,20]. Fig. 2 (D) shows the typical XRD pattern of GQDs which displays a prominent peak around 24.5 corresponds to the 0.363 nm interlayer spacing and is assigned to graphite (002) planes [21].

The morphology of the synthesized GQDs was first observed and characterized using high-resolution TEM as shown in Fig. 2 (E). The GQDs display the spherical dot structure. The GQDs lattice spacing was measured to be 0.18 nm. The TEM image depicted in Fig. 2 (F) assures the high crystalline nature of the GQDs. The FESEM analysis of the electrodeposited gold film was conducted. The electrodeposited gold layer displays the flower-like morphology as visible in Fig. S(2). The FESEM illustration of the electropolymerized layer of the PEDOT before and after the extraction of the BPA is shown in Fig. 2 (G) and (H) respectively. The extracted layer shows the more dispersed morphology compared to the non-extracted layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalytical evaluation of the molecularly imprinted sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical response at various stages of modification of the electrode was recorded and shown in Fig. S3 . The scan rate studies were also performed to show the linearity of the fabricated electrode’s current response and the scan rate. The obtained linear results of the scan rate are displayed in Fig. S4 . DPV was used to study the current responses of various concentrations of the BPA. The concentration study of the BPA was performed within a concentration range of 1 nM to 50 µM. The whole concentration study was performed in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl and 0.005 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. From the DPV graph as shown in Fig. 3
(A) it was clear that the peak current response rises significantly with an increasing concentration of the BPA. The calibration plot was plotted between the response of the current and the various concentrations. Graph Fig. 3 (B) displays two linear ranges between the log concentrations and change in current, one from the range 1 nM to 250 nM and another from 500 nM to 50 µM. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to be 0.19 nM and 0.64 nM respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 (C), the imprinted electrode displays a prominent peak of BPA at the potential of ∼ 0.5 V, whereas in the case of the non-imprinted electrode Fig. 3 (D), no significant peak of BPA was observed. This proves the high specificity of the MIP electrode. The stability and selectivity results are displayed in Fig. S5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of BPA in spiked tap water
ce:section.ce:para: A tap water sample was collected and the electrochemical studies were performed by spiking the known concentrations of the BPA. The recovery percentage was found to be ranging between 95 and 112 as given in Table 1 . The comparison of the sensor for the determination of BPA along with the previously reported work is tabulated in Table S1 . These results show the applicability of the fabricated electrode for the real sample analysis.

In this study, an electrochemical sensor based on MIP was created to detect bisphenol-A. On the surface of the GCE, the MIP-based sensor was created using the electropolymerization approach. The conductive polymer, 3–4 ethylenedioxythiophene was used as a monomer. The complementary imprinted cavities with a high affinity for the BPA are left behind when the template is removed from the electropolymerized MIP layer. The sensor showed a wide linear range with a low detection limit of 0.19 nM and a limit of quantification of 0.64 nM. The applicability of the fabricated sensor was also validated with the tap water samples. Future point-of-care devices for the detection of bisphenol-A may be created using the developed MIP-based sensor.

Ayushi Singhal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Raju Khan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Visualization, Data curation, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the director of CSIR-AMPRI for his assistance and encouragement in this project. Raju Khan would like to thank SERB for funding the IPA/2020/000130 project with cash.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134880.

ce:section.ce:para:
